translations.source: Ukwenza okuphuthumayo kudinga kubhekwane nakho kulokhu kumiswa kokuqhubeka kokuhlobene nezempilo kwe Sustainable Development Goals I-WHO izokhipha uhlobo lwa-2023 lombiko wonyaka weWorld Health Statistics nezibalo ezintsha kumthelela wobhubhane leCOVID-19 kanye nezibalo zakamuva ekuqhubekeni okuya kokuhlobene nezempilo zeSustainable Development Goals (amaSDG). Umbiko wemininingo engafika ku-2022 ekala ukuma kokuqhubeka kwezempilo kuzinkomba zezempilo ezibalulekile eminyakeni yakamuva uma uqhathanisa nokudumile okubonakele ngesikhathi sika-2000-2015. Kuphinde kuqaphelise kuthina ukukhula kokusabisa kwezifo okungaxhunyanwa ngazo (amaNCD) nokuguquka kwesimo sezulu nezingcingo zokuxhumana nokuqinisa impendulo. I-COVID-19 ibiza izimpilo ezilahlekile nokuqhubeka kwezempilo amadokhumenti ombiko yezibalo ezibuyekeziwe esiqongweni sobhubhane lezempilo yomhlaba,ukunikela ekuqhubekeni kokwehla kokuqhubeka okuya kumaSDG. Ngesikhathi sa-2020-2021 iCOVID-19 ilethe ukuhamba kancane izigidi ezingu-336.8 eminyakeni yempilo elahlekile emhlabeni. Lokhu kulinganisa isilinganiso seminyaka engu-22 yempilo elahlekile kukhona konke ukufa okwenzekile, ubunzima bokusheshe uqede izimpilo eziyizigidi zabantu. Kusukela ngo-2000, sibone ukuthuthuka okukhulu kwezempilo zomama nezingane nokushona okwehla ikota eyodwa nohhafu, ngokulandelana. Isigameko sezifo ezithelelanayo njengeHIV, isifo sofuba (TB) kanye nesifo sikamalaleveva siphinde sehla kanye nokwehla kwengcuphe yokufa kwabasheshe bazalwa eNCD kanye nokulimala. Ndawonye, lokhu kube nomthelela ekunyukeni kwesikhathi sokuphila emhlabeni kusuka ku-67 iminyaka ngo-2000 kuya ku-73 iminyaka ngo-2019.
translations.reference: Urgent action needed to tackle stalled progress on health-related Sustainable Development Goals WHO is releasing the 2023 edition of its annual World Health Statistics report with new figures on the impact of COVID-19 pandemic and the latest statistics on progress towards the health-related Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). The report with data up to 2022 underscores a stagnation of health progress on key health indicators in recent years compared with trends seen during 2000-2015. It also alerts us to the growing threat of noncommunicable diseases (NCDs) and climate change, and calls for a coordinated and strengthened response. COVID-19 cost in lost lives and health progress The report documents updated statistics on the toll of the pandemic on global health, contributing to the ongoing decline in progress towards the SDGs. During 2020-2021, COVID-19 resulted in a staggering 336.8 million years of life lost globally. This equates to an average of 22 years of life lost for every excess death, abruptly and tragically cutting short the lives of millions of people. Since 2000, we saw significant improvements in maternal and child health with deaths falling by one-third and one-half, respectively. The incidence of infectious diseases such as HIV, tuberculosis (TB) and malaria also declined, along with a lowered risk of premature deaths from NCDs and injuries. Together, these contributed to an increase in global life expectancy from 67 years in 2000 to 73 years in 2019.
translations.hypothesis: Responding quickly to the ongoing health challenges requires a comprehensive and coordinated approach, particularly in relation to the Sustainable Development Goals. The World Health Organization (WHO) will release its 2023 edition of the World Health Statistics report, along with updated data on the COVID-19 pandemic and progress towards the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). The report, which covers data up to 2022, highlights the continued impact of the pandemic on health outcomes, particularly in the years following the 2000-2015 baseline. It also addresses the growing burden of non-communicable diseases (NCDs) and the impact of climate change and environmental factors on health. The COVID-19 pandemic has caused significant loss of life and has disrupted progress towards the SDGs. In 2020-2021, the pandemic resulted in an estimated 336.8 years of life lost globally. This represents a significant setback in achieving the goal of increasing life expectancy by 22 years, which was a key target of the SDGs. Since 2000, there has been significant progress in reducing mortality rates from maternal and child health, as well as from infectious diseases such as HIV, tuberculosis, and malaria. However, progress has stalled in some areas, particularly in the reduction of NCD mortality and disability. Overall, the pandemic has had a significant impact on global life expectancy, which fell from 67 years in 2000 to 73 years in 2019.

translations.source: Kepha ke, ubhubhane selubeke izinkomba eziningi ezihlobene nezempilo ukuqhubeka ukuchezuka nokunikela ekungalinganeni kokuthola ukunakekelwa kwezempilo okusezingeni eliphezulu, izindlela zokugoma kanye nokuvikelwa kwezomphakathi. Njengomphumela, ukwenzangcono okudumile kwamalaleveva neTB sekuhlehlisiwe nabantu abambalwa balashwa belashelwa izifo ezidumile ezinganakiwe (amaNTD). "I-World Health Statistics kuwukubheka kweminyaka kweWHO esimweni sezempilo yomhlaba. Umbiko uthumela umyalezo ekwesabeni izifo okungaxhunyanwa ngazo,okuthatha okukhulu nokunyuka ezimpilweni zabantu,ukuphila,izinhlelo zempilo ,imiphakathi ezomnotho nemiphakathi," kusho uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus uMqondisi Jikelele weWHO. "Izimemezelo zombiko zokunyuka okungagcineka kutshalomali lwezempilo ukubuyela esimweni seSustainable Development Goals." Ama-NCD-ahlezi enyusa ukwesabela ezempilo zesizukulwane sakusasa, Ngaphandle kwakho konke ukuqhubeka, ukwabelana ngokufa okudalwe ngonyaka amaNCD sekukhule njalo futhi manje sekuthathe amakota amathathu azo zonke izimpilo ezilahlekile unyaka ngamunye. Uma lokhu kujwayeleka kuqhubeka, amaNCD azothatha icala le-86% wokufa kwezigidi ezingu-90 ngonyaka phakathi kweminyaka eyikhulu, ngokulandelana izigidi ezingu-77 zalokhu ngezeNCD -kungafinyelela ku-90% ukwenyuka kwezinamba kusukela ngo-2019. Ukuhamba kancane kokuqhubeka kwezimemezelo zokuqhubeka okudumile kwakamuva kukhombisa izinkomba zokuhamba kancane kwezinga lonyaka lokwehla kwezinkomba eziningi (ARR). Isibonelo isilinganiso sokufa komama emhlabeni sidinga ukwehla ngo-11.6% ngonykaka phakathi kuka-2021 no-2030 ukuhlangabezana nokuqondwe yiSDG. Ngokufanayo, ukwehla konke endabeni yeTB kusuka ngo-2015 kuya ku-2021 kwakungamakota amahlanu kosekuhanjiwe ngo-2025 kweWHO END TB Strategy.
translations.reference: However, the pandemic has put many health-related indicators further off-track and contributed to inequalities in access to high-quality health care, routine immunizations and financial protection. As a result, improving trends in malaria and TB have been reversed, and fewer people were treated for neglected tropical diseases (NTDs). “The World Health Statistics is WHO’s annual check-up on the state of the world’s health. The report sends a stark message on the threat of noncommunicable diseases, which take an immense and increasing toll on lives, livelihoods, health systems, communities, economies and societies,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. “The report calls for a substantial increase in investments in health and health systems to get back on track towards the Sustainable Development Goals.” NCDs ̶ an ever-increasing health threat for future generationsDespite overall health progress, the share of deaths caused annually by NCDs has grown consistently and is now claiming nearly three quarters of all lives lost each year. If this trend continues, NCDs are projected to account for about 86% of the 90 million annual deaths by mid-century; consequently, 77 million of these will be due to NCDs – a nearly 90% increase in absolute numbers since 2019. Stagnating progress calls for acceleration More recent trends show signs of slowdown in the annual rate of reduction (ARR) for many indicators. For example, the global maternal mortality ratio needs to decline by 11.6% per year between 2021 and 2030 to meet the SDG target. Similarly, the net reduction in TB incidence from 2015 to 2021 was only one-fifth of the way to the 2025 milestone of WHO’s End TB Strategy.
translations.hypothesis: However, the global health landscape continues to present significant challenges, including the ongoing spread of infectious diseases, the rise of non-communicable diseases (NCDs), and the need for improved health systems and social protection. As a result, progress towards achieving universal health coverage (UHC) has been uneven, with some countries making significant gains while others continue to lag behind. "The World Health Statistics report provides a comprehensive overview of the global health situation, highlighting key trends and challenges, including the burden of infectious diseases, the growing prevalence of NCDs, and the need for stronger health systems," said Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of WHO. "The report also emphasizes the importance of investing in sustainable development goals to achieve UHC." The rise of NCDs poses a major threat to global health, as they are the leading cause of death worldwide, accounting for over 70% of all deaths. Without further action, the number of deaths from NCDs is projected to continue to rise, reaching over 90 million per year by 2030. This would represent a significant increase from the current level of over 77 million. If this trend continues, NCDs could account for 86% of all deaths by 2030.

translations.source: Ngaphandle kokwehla ekubonakaleni kwengcuphe yezempilo eningi-njengokusetshenziswa kwagwayi, ukuphuza utshwala, udlame,amanzi angaphephile nokuhlanzeka, okwezingane, ukuqhubeka nokunganeli nokuvuleleka kwezinye izingcuphe njengokungcola komoya kuhlezi kuphezulu. Okuthusayo, ukusabalala kokukhuluphala ngokweqile kuyanyuka kungekho nophawu lokukubuyisela emuva. Ukuqhubekaukwanda kokuthola emisebenzini yezempilo ebalulekile sekwehlile uma uqhathanisa kokuzuza kwaphambili kwa-2015, okuhlanganiswe nokuqhubeka okukhulu ekwehliseni ubunzima kwezezimali ngenxa yokubiza kokunakekelwa kwezempilo. Imikhawulo emikhulu yokukwazi kwethu ukuthola i-Universal Health Coverage ngo-2030. "Ubhubhane lweCOVID-19 luyisikhumbuzo esibalulekile sokuthi ukuqhubeka akuhambi ngolayini noma akuqinisekisiwe," kuxwayisa uDkt Samira Asma, Umsizi woMqondisi Jikele weMininingo kuWHO, ukuhlaziya nokulethwa kwemithelela. "Ukuhlala esimweni okuya kuAjenda yeSDG 2030, kumele sinqume kanhle silethe ngokubambisana kumthelela okaliwe kuwona wonke amazwe." Umbiko wakulonyaka ufaka okokuqala isahluko sokuzinikela kwezokuguquka kwesimo sezulu nezempilo, futhi siyakubona lokhu kuzohlobana embikweni kusuka manje kuya phambili. Ngalenkinga nazo zonke izindawo,ukwethembeka kanye nokungesona isilinganisso solwazi kubucayi ukulandelela ukuqhubeka nokwenza ngcono ezweni nakuzinqubomgomo zezempilo emhlabeni. Inothi lomhleli: iWolrd Health Statistics umbiko yiWolrd Health Organisation (WHO) ukuhlanganisa konyaka kolwazi olukhona lwakamuva kwezempilo kanye nezinkomba ezihlobene nezempilo. Uma unemibuzo xhumana [imeyili ivikelekile]
translations.reference: Despite a reduction in exposure to many health risks – such as tobacco use, alcohol consumption, violence, unsafe water and sanitation, and child stunting – progress was inadequate and exposure to some risks such as air pollution remains high. Alarmingly, the prevalence of obesity is rising with no immediate sign of reversal. Furthermore, expanded access to essential health services has slowed compared to pre-2015 gains, coupled with no significant progress in reducing financial hardship due to health-care costs. This drastically limits our ability to achieve Universal Health Coverage by 2030. “The COVID-19 pandemic is an important reminder that progress is neither linear nor guaranteed,” warns Dr Samira Asma, WHO Assistant Director-General for Data, Analytics and Delivery for Impact. “To stay on track towards the 2030 SDG agenda, we must act decisively and collectively to deliver a measurable impact in all countries.” This year’s report includes for the first time a dedicated section on climate change and health, and we anticipate that this will be of more relevance in the report going forward. For this issue and all other areas timely, reliable and disaggregated data are critical to track progress and improve national and global health policies. Editor’s note: The World Health Statistics report is the World Health Organization’s (WHO) annual compilation of the most recent available data on health and health-related indicators. For inquiries, contact [email protected]
translations.hypothesis: In addition to the decline in indicators of health such as vaccination rates, access to clean water, sanitation, and healthcare, there has also been a decrease in other important health indicators, including maternal mortality rates and child mortality rates. Furthermore, the spread of misinformation and disinformation is a growing concern. The COVID-19 pandemic has highlighted the importance of continued investment in global health, and it is clear that progress towards achieving universal health coverage by 2030 will be challenging. "The COVID-19 pandemic is a stark reminder that progress is not guaranteed and that we must remain vigilant," warns Dr. Samira Asma, Deputy Director-General of the World Health Organization (WHO), on the importance of continued investment in global health. "To achieve the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), we must continue to work together to ensure that everyone has access to the health services they need." This year's report marks the beginning of a new era of global health, and it is clear that this will be a major focus in the years to come. In light of these challenges, it is more important than ever to ensure that we are investing in global health and that we are working together to achieve the SDGs.

translations.source: Umqondisi Jikelele weWHO umemezele Imiklomelo yaBaholi beZempilo eMhlabeni UJean-Jacques Muyembe-Tamfum noPeter Piot baklonyelwe ngokuzibophezela impilo yabo yonke kwezempilo Umqondisi Jikelele weWHO uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus uzokwethula imiklomelo yempilo yonke emibili ukubona ukunikela okumangazayo ukwenza ngcono ezempilo zomhlaba ngesikhathi seseshini yokuvula esezingeni eliphakeme yoMhlangano weZempilo woMhlaba wamashumi ayisithupha nesithupha. Imiklomelo yaBaholi boMhlaba yaMqondisi Jikelele, eyasungulwa ngo-2019 izoya kuJean-Jacques Muyembe-Tamfum noPeter Piot. Bobabili abatholile bangosayensi abahlukile futhi bangabaholi bezempilo yomphakathi ababezibandakanye kakhulu ekutholeni isifo segciwane ibhola ngaphambi kokudlulela phambili ngesikhundla kwezobuholi kwezempilo emhlabeni. Umklomelo ubahlonipha ngabakuzuze impilo yabo yonke kwezempilo yomphakathi. USolwazi Muyembe-Tamfum waseDemocratic Republic of Congo (DRC) uyabonwa ngokwenza okusha, ubuholi bakhe nomnikelo omangazayo kuya ekuphatheni nokwelapha izifo ezinjenge sifo segciwane i-Ebola. Usedlale indima ebucayi ekwenzeni ngcono imiphumela yezempilo eDemocratic Republic of Congo, isifunda sase-Afrika kanye nomphakathi wasemhlabeni. USolwazi Muyembe-Tamfum unguMqondisi Jikelele weDRC National Institute of Biomedical Research eKinshasa, uSolwazi ogcwele weMicrobiology eNyuvesi yaseKinshasa Medical School nomongameli wokwethula waseCongolese Academy of Science. USolwazi Muyembe-Tamfum wayeyingxenye yeqembu elathola ukuthi iZaire inegciwane lebhola ngesikhathi kuqoshwa okokuqala ukubheduka ngo-1976 eSifundazweni saseEquateur, Maphakathi neDRC.
translations.reference: WHO Director-General announces Global Health Leaders Awards Jean-Jacques Muyembe-Tamfum and Peter Piot awarded for lifelong commitment to health WHO Director-General Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus will present two lifetime achievement awards to recognize outstanding contributions to advancing global health during the high-level opening session of the Seventy-sixth World Health Assembly. The Director-General’s Global Leaders Awards, which were established in 2019, will go to Jean-Jacques Muyembe-Tamfum and Peter Piot. Both recipients are distinguished scientists and public health leaders who were closely involved in the discovery of Ebola virus disease before advancing to leadership positions in global health. The award honours their lifetime achievements in public health. Professor Muyembe-Tamfum from the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) is being recognized for his innovation, leadership and ground-breaking contributions to the management and treatment of diseases such as Ebola virus disease. He has played a critical role in improving health outcomes in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, the African region, and the global community. Professor Muyembe-Tamfum is the Director General of the DRC’s National Institute of Biomedical Research in Kinshasa, full Professor of Microbiology at the University of Kinshasa Medical School and the inaugural president of the Congolese Academy of Science. Professor Muyembe-Tamfum was part of the team that discovered the Zaïre ebolavirus during the first recorded outbreak in 1976, in Equateur Province, Central DRC.
translations.hypothesis:
The Director-General of the WHO, Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, will present two prestigious health awards to recognize the outstanding contributions of two global health leaders: Dr. Jean-Jacques Muyembe-Tamfum and Dr. Peter Piot. The awards, established in 2019, will honor Dr. Muyembe-Tamfum and Dr. Piot for their remarkable achievements in advancing global health.

Dr. Muyembe-Tamfum, from the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), is a renowned scientist and public health leader who has made significant contributions to the fight against Ebola and other infectious diseases. He played a crucial role in the DRC's response to the Ebola outbreak in 2018, and his expertise in epidemiology and public health has been invaluable to the country's efforts to improve health outcomes.

Dr. Piot, from Belgium, is a leading expert in infectious diseases and a former Director of the WHO's Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria. He has dedicated his career to improving global health, and his work has had a profound impact on the lives of millions of people around the world.

translations.source: Makhathaleni waseza ukwakha omunye wemishanguzo youqala esebenzayo ekulweni negciwane nokufakwa kokuhlolwa kwemigomo yesifo segciwane i-Ebola. Usesebenza ngokungakhathali ukwakha iDemocratic Republic of the Congo emandleni esayensi, ukwakha izindawo zokwenza ucwaningo oluningi ezweni, usedlale indima ebaluleke kakhulu neWHO ekulweni nezifo ezithelelanayo. USolwazi Piot waseBelgium uyabonakala ngobuholi obuhle kakhulu ekuphenduleni ngobhubhane lweHIV/AIDS kanye nezinye izingqinamba ezibalulekile zezempilo emhlabeni. Usedlale indima ebaluleke kakhulu ekwethulweni kwemizamo emikhulu yezempilo emhlabeni njenge-UNAIDS, iGlobal Fund, kanye neminye esiyenze ngcono imiphumela yezempilo emhlabeni wonke. Ngaphansi kobuholi bakhe, i-UNAIDS ibe ngabameli abakhulu emhlabeni wonke bokwenza okubhekene neAIDS kanye nokukhomba indlela ekwenzeni busha i-UN ngokuhlanganisa ndawonye izinhlangano ezahlukene ezingu-10 ze-UN. USolwazi Piot ube yingxenye futhi yokusheshe kutholakale igciwane i-Ebola, nokulilawula ngesikhathi libheduka okokuqala lingaziwa, ukubamba iqhaza ekwenzeni ucwaningo ngezobhubhane. Usebe ngusihlalo wemizamo eminingi esezingeni eliphezulu,wasebenza emazingeni aphezulu amaningi njengomeluleki, okufaka nakuCOVID-19 usethole imiklomelo eminingi. USolwazi Piot unguMeluleki okhethekile kuMongameli wase-EC, uSolwazi weZempilo yoMhlaba owayenguMqondisi weLondon School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. "Laba bantu abahlabahlosile banokuzinikela impilo yonke, ukumela, ukuzibophezela ekulinganeni, ukusebenzela abantu kanye nezempilo zabantu," kusho uMqondisi Jikelele weWHO, uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. "Ngesikhathi sokuphuthumayo okuningi,izinkinga zezempilo nokuxabana,siyabubona ubuholi kwezempilo yomphakathi nesayensi kubaluleke kakhulu ukwenzangcono ezempilo emhlabeni.
translations.reference: He later helped design one of the first effective treatments against the virus and the deployment of experimental Ebola virus disease vaccines. He has worked tirelessly to build the Democratic Republic of the Congo’s scientific capacity, establishing multiple research facilities in the country, and has played key roles with WHO in fighting infectious diseases. Professor Piot from Belgium is being recognized for his extraordinary leadership in the response to the HIV/AIDS epidemic and other significant global health challenges. He has played a vital role in launching major global health initiatives such as UNAIDS, the Global Fund, and others that have improved health outcomes around the world. Under his leadership, UNAIDS became the chief advocate for worldwide action against AIDS, also spearheading UN reform by bringing together 10 different UN organizations. Professor Piot also played a part in the earlier discovery of the Ebola virus, and the containment of the first known outbreak, participating in field epidemiological investigations. He has chaired a number of high-level initiatives, served in numerous high-level advisory roles, including on COVID-19, and has received multiple awards. Professor Piot is the Special Advisor to the EC President, a Professor of Global Health and former Director of the London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine. “These outstanding individuals embody lifelong dedication, relentless advocacy, a commitment to equity, and selfless service of humanity and people’s health,” said WHO Director-General, Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. “In a time of multiple emergencies, health crises, and conflict, we recognize that leadership in public health and science are more important than ever to advance global health.
translations.hypothesis: Makhathaleni was instrumental in establishing one of the first operational research centers for the diagnosis and treatment of Ebola and other tropical diseases. He played a crucial role in strengthening the Democratic Republic of the Congo's scientific capacity, establishing research facilities, and collaborating with the WHO on disease control efforts. Dr. Piot has been a leading figure in the global response to HIV/AIDS and other major public health challenges. He has been instrumental in the development of major global health initiatives, such as UNAIDS, the Global Fund, and other efforts to improve health outcomes worldwide. Under his leadership, UNAIDS has become a leading global body for coordinating the response to AIDS and advocating for the rights of people living with the disease. Dr. Piot was also a key figure in the rapid development and deployment of the Ebola vaccine, and he has been involved in research on other infectious diseases. He has served as a senior advisor to numerous organizations, including the World Health Organization, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, and the National Institutes of Health. Dr. Piot is a distinguished professor at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, and he is a member of the World Health Organization's Executive Board. "These individuals are essential to the health and well-being of all people, and their work is vital to creating a healthier and more equitable world," said Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of the WHO.

translations.source: Abahlonishwa bethu namhlanje bakhombisa leyo ntshisekelo." "Izindaba zabo behlangene nokuphumelela kukhombisa ukuthi umhlaba usuqhubeke kangakanani ngokokubambisana emhlabeni,esifundeni nasemazingeni ezwe ukuqinisa ukuzilungiselela okuphuthumayo kwezempilo nokuphendula nokubekezela. Amasayensisi agqamile azoqhubeka ukugqugquzela zonke izinsuku," wengezile emcimbini wemiklomelo njengengxenye yeseshini yokuvula esezingeni eliphezulu lesi-76th World Health Assembly ozobanjwa ngesonto mhlaka-21 May 2023 eGeneva.
translations.reference: Our awardees today personify that passion.” “Their combined stories and successes show how much the world has progressed in terms of collaboration at the global, regional and country levels to strengthen health emergency preparedness, response and resilience. These outstanding scientists will continue to inspire us every day,” he added The ceremony for the awards form part of the high-level opening session of the 76th World Health Assembly being held on Sunday 21 May 2023 in Geneva.
translations.hypothesis: Our distinguished guests today have highlighted this important issue. "Their reports and success stories demonstrate that the world is continuing to make progress in collaboration, particularly in areas of global health, education, and humanitarian aid, to strengthen preparedness for future health emergencies. These dedicated scientists will continue to make significant contributions," she concluded at the awards ceremony as part of the opening session of the 76th World Health Assembly, which will be held on May 21, 2023, in Geneva.

translations.source: I-WHO ihalalisela iBenin neMali ngokuqeda isifo samehlo njengenkinga yezempilo yomphakathi I-WHO isiqinisekise iBenin neMali ngokuqda isifo samehlo njengenkinga yezempilo yomphakathi,nokubenza abesihlanu nesithupha amazwe aseAfrika eWHO ukuthola umsebenzi omkhulu kangaka. Amazwe asethole ukugunyazwa okuvela kwiWHO ngokuqeda isifo samehlo yiGhana (ngoNhlangulana 2018) iGambia (ngoMbasa 2021) Togo (ngoNhlaba 2022 kanye neMalawi (ngoMandulo 2022). "WHO ihalalisela abasemagunyeni bezempilo baseBenin neMali kanye nohlelokuxhumana lwabo emhlabeni kanye nababambisani bendawo kulokhu asebekuzuzile," kusho uDkt Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus uMqondisi Jikelele weWHO. "Kulandela impumelelo yeBenin neMali isifo samehlo siyaqhubeka siba wubhubhane emazweni angu-23 eWHO African Region, kusiletha eduze kokuqedwa okuhlosiwe okuhleliwe kwesifo samehlo kubalazwe kulezofifo ezijwaelekile ezinganakiwe 2021-2030." Emhlabeni iBenin neMali bazojoyina amanye amazwe angu-15 asegunyazwe yiWHO ngokuqeda isifo samehlo njengenkinga yezempilo yomphakathi. Lawo mazwe yiCambodia, iChina, iGambia, iGhana, i-Islamic Republic of Iran, iLao Peoples Democratic Republic, iMalawi, iMexico, iMorocco, iMyanmar, iNepal, i-Oman, iSaudi Arabia, iTogo neVanuatu. Ukuqeda isifo samehlo usebenzisa isu leSAFE leWHO Womabili amazwe iBenin neMali benze isiphakamiso sesu lokuphepha leWHO ukuqeda isifo samehlo ngokwesekwa yiWHO nababambisani. Isu leSAFE liqukethe ukuhlinzwa ukuze welaphe isifo samehlo ngobunzia,ama-antibhayothikhi ukuqeda ukungcola,izinto zokuhlanza ubuso,ukwenzangcono indawo,ikakhulukazi ukwenza ngcono ukuthola amazi nokuthuthwa kwendle ukwehlisa ukudlulisela.
translations.reference: WHO congratulates Benin and Mali for eliminating trachoma as a public health problem WHO has validated Benin and Mali as having eliminated trachoma as a public health problem, making them the fifth and sixth countries in WHO’s African Region to achieve this significant milestone. Countries that previously received WHO validation for trachoma elimination are Ghana (June 2018), Gambia (April 2021), Togo (May 2022) and Malawi (September 2022). “WHO congratulates the health authorities of Benin and Mali and their network of global and local partners for these milestones”, said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. “Following Benin’s and Mali’s success, trachoma remains endemic in 23 countries in WHO’s African Region, bringing us a step closer towards the elimination target for trachoma set in the road map for neglected tropical diseases 2021–2030.” Globally, Benin and Mali join 15 other countries that have been validated by WHO for having eliminated trachoma as a public health problem. These are Cambodia, China, the Gambia, Ghana, Islamic Republic of Iran, Lao People’s Democratic Republic, Malawi, Mexico, Morocco, Myanmar, Nepal, Oman, Saudi Arabia, Togo and Vanuatu. Trachoma elimination using WHO’s SAFE strategy Both Benin and Mali implemented the WHO-recommended SAFE strategy to eliminate trachoma with the support of WHO and partners. The SAFE strategy consists of surgery to treat late trachoma complications; antibiotics to clear infection; facial cleanliness; and environmental improvement, particularly improving access to water and sanitation, to reduce transmission.
translations.hypothesis: The WHO has praised Benin and Mali for their efforts to eliminate trachoma as a public health priority. The WHO has recognized Benin and Mali for their progress in eliminating trachoma as a public health priority, making them the fifth and sixth countries in Africa to achieve this milestone. The two countries received recognition from the WHO for their efforts to eliminate trachoma in 2018, 2021, 2022, and 2022, respectively. "I am pleased to commend the Ministries of Health in Benin and Mali, as well as their partners, for their commitment to eliminating trachoma," said Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of the WHO. "Following the success of Benin and Mali, trachoma is on track to be eliminated as a public health problem in the WHO African Region by 2021, as outlined in the WHO's Global Elimination Goal for trachoma. Globally, Benin and Mali join 15 other countries that have been recognized by the WHO for their efforts to eliminate trachoma as a public health priority. These countries include Cambodia, China, Gambia, Ghana, Islamic Republic of Iran, Lao Peoples Democratic Republic, Malawi, Mexico, Morocco, Myanmar, Nepal, Oman, Saudi Arabia, Togo, and Vanuatu. The WHO's SAFE strategy, which stands for surgical repair, antibiotic treatment, facial cleanliness, and environmental improvement, has been instrumental in the success of Benin and Mali's trachoma elimination efforts.

translations.source: Ngomzamo wesifo samehlo wamazwe angaphandle,antibhayothikhi azithromycin xhase ngePfizer ekuqedweni kwezinhlelo zokwenza zesu leSAFE. I-Benin isihlanganise isifo samehlo ukuqedwa nokungenelela kulabo abaenza okubhekene nezinye izifo ezijwayelekile ezinganakiwe (amaNTD) ngaphansi kwesambulela seNationa Programme for Communicable Disesases. Isifo samehlo ngesesithathu kuNTD ukuqedwa eBenin emva kwedracunculiasis (ngo-2009) negambiese yabantu eAfrika trypanosomiasis (ngo-2021). Imali isicwaninge umthelela wesifo samehlo nocwaningo lokubheka nokukhipha izingenelelo ukuthola okuqondwe ukuqedwa,ngaphandle kwezingqinamba zokuphepha ezifundeni zasenyakatho yezwe nezinkinga zepolitiki yezenhlalakahle eminyakeni yakamuva nje. Isifo samehlo ngesokuqala seNTD ukuqedwa eMali ngakho manje esijoyina iqoqo lomhlaba lamazwe angu-47 aseqede okungenani esisodwa seNTD. "Lokhu ukuzuza kwezempilo yomphakathi okujabulisayo," kusho uDkt Ibrahima Soce Fall uMqondisi woHlelo lweNTD eMhlabeni lweWHO. "I-Benin nemali ikhombisileukuthi kuqine kanjani isifiso sezepolitiki,ukuhlangana kwemikhakha eyahlukene,ukubheka nokukhuluma komphakathi kungasebenza kusivumelwano sokuthola ukuqedwa kwesio." Ukuqhubeka okubalulekile sekwenzekile ekulweni okubhekene nesifo samehlo eminyakeni embalwa edlule. Abantu abaningi badinga kwelashwa nge antibhayothikhi ukuqeda isifo samehlo eWHO Afrikan Region abafika ezigidini ezingu-84 kusuka kuzigidi ezingu-189 ngo-2014 kuya kuzigidi ezingu-105 kusuka ngoNhlangulana 2022. Inothi eliya kumhleli: Isifo samehlo (Trachoma) sizohlala siyinkinga yezempilo yomphakathi emazweni angu-41 (kusuka ngoNhlangulana 2022) ngesibalo esilinganiselwe kuzigidi ezingu-125 zabantu abaphila ezindaweni ezidinga ukungenelela okubhekene nalesifo.
translations.reference: Through the International Trachoma Initiative, the antibiotic azithromycin is donated by Pfizer to elimination programmes implementing the SAFE strategy. Benin has integrated trachoma elimination interventions with those implemented against other neglected tropical diseases (NTDs), under the umbrella of the National Programme for Communicable Diseases. Trachoma is the third NTD to be eliminated in Benin, after dracunculiasis (in 2009) and gambiense human African trypanosomiasis (in 2021). Mali has conducted trachoma impact and surveillance surveys and rolled out interventions to achieve elimination targets, despite security challenges in the northern regions of the country and sociopolitical upheavals in recent years. Trachoma is the first NTD to be eliminated in Mali, which therefore now joins a global group of 47 countries that have eliminated at least one NTD. “These are impressive public health achievements,” said Dr Ibrahima Socé Fall, Director of the WHO Global NTD Programme. “Benin and Mali demonstrate how strong political will, cross-sector integration, surveillance and community engagement can work in concert to achieve disease elimination.” Significant progress has been made in the fight against trachoma over the past few years. The number of people requiring antibiotic treatment for trachoma in the WHO African Region fell by 84 million, from 189 million in 2014 to 105 million as of June 2022. Note to the editor: Trachoma remains a public health problem in 41 countries (as of June 2022) with an estimated 125 million people living in areas requiring interventions against the disease.
translations.hypothesis: In response to a global health crisis, the government of Benin has launched a new campaign to combat trachoma, a leading cause of preventable blindness. The campaign aims to reduce the prevalence of trachoma and other neglected tropical diseases (NTDs) through a comprehensive approach that includes mass drug administration (MDA) with azithromycin, provided by Pfizer, as part of the SAFE program. Trachoma is the third leading NTD in Benin, after trachoma (2009) and lymphatic filariasis (2021). The government is investing in trachoma control and elimination efforts, despite challenges related to security and political instability in recent years. Trachoma is the first NTD to be eliminated in Mali, and the country is now part of the global effort to eliminate this disease. "This is a remarkable achievement for public health," said Dr. Ibrahim Soce Fall, Director of the NTD Program at the WHO African Region. "The success of Benin and Mali demonstrates that with strong political commitment, collaboration between different sectors, and community engagement, we can achieve the elimination of trachoma." Progress has been made in controlling trachoma in recent years. The number of people requiring treatment for trachoma in the WHO African Region decreased from 189 million in 2014 to 105 million by December 2022.

translations.source: Isifo samehlo sitholakala kakhulu ezindaweni ezingamakhaya futhi ezihluphekayo e-Afrika, Maphakathi naseNingizimu neAmerica, Asia naseWestern Pacific kanye neMiddle East. I-WHO African Region itheleleke yisifo samehlo kubantu abayizigidi ezingu 105 abahlala ezindaweni eziyingcuphe,ezimele u-48% womthwalo wesifo samehlo emhlabeni. Isifo samehlo isona esihamba phambili ekuthelelaneni esidala ukungaboni emhlabeni wonke. Isifo sidalwa ukuthelelana ngegciwane iChlamydia trachomatis. Ukuthelelana kudluliseka kumuntu kuye kumuntu ngeminwe engcolile, izinambuzane, izimpukane esezihlangabezane nokungcola okuphuma emahlweni noma ikhala lomuntu othelelekile. Izinkinga zengcephe yendawo yokudluliselwa kwesifo samehlo okufaka ukungahlanzeki,imizi egcwele ukungatholi kahle amanzi nokusebenzisa izindawo zokuzikhulula ezifanele. Ngo-1996, iWHO yethula iWHO Alliane yokuqedwa kwesifo samehlo emhlabeni ngonyaka ka-2020 (GET2020). Nabanye ababambisani kwi-Alliance, iWHO yeseka ukwenza kwezwe kweSu lokuPhepha nokuqinisa amandla ezwe ngokhlolwa kwezobhubhane, ukubheka, ukuhlola, ukubhekisa iphrojekthi nokuhlanganisa izinsizakusebenza ezizosiza ekuqedweni kwesifo samehlo njengenkinga yezempilo yomphakathi. Umgwaqo balazwe ogunyazwe nguMhlangano weZempilo eMhlabeni ngo-2020 ngesinqumo sawo 73(33) ibeka u-2030 njengosuku oluqondiwe olusha lokuqeda emhlabeni.
translations.reference: Trachoma is found mainly in the poorest and most rural areas of Africa, Central and South America, Asia, the Western Pacific and the Middle East. The WHO African Region is disproportionately affected by trachoma with 105 million people living in at-risk areas, which represents 84% of the global trachoma burden. Trachoma is the leading infectious cause of blindness worldwide. The disease is caused by infection with the bacterium Chlamydia trachomatis. Infection is transmitted from person to person through contaminated fingers, fomites and flies that have come into contact with discharge from the eyes or nose of an infected person. Environmental risk factors for trachoma transmission include poor hygiene, overcrowded households, inadequate access to water or use of proper sanitation facilities. In 1996, WHO launched the WHO Alliance for the Global Elimination of Trachoma by the year 2020 (GET2020). With other partners in the Alliance, WHO supports country implementation of the SAFE strategy and strengthening of national capacity through epidemiological assessment, monitoring, surveillance, project evaluation and resource mobilization contributing towards elimination of trachoma as a public health problem. The road map, endorsed by the World Health Assembly in 2020 through its decision 73(33), sets 2030 as the new target date for global elimination.
translations.hypothesis: Trachoma, a form of blindness, is most prevalent in rural and underserved areas of Africa, as well as in the Americas, Asia, and the Western Pacific and Middle East. The World Health Organization (WHO) estimates that over 105 million people are affected by trachoma globally, representing 48% of the total global burden of trachoma. Trachoma is the leading cause of preventable blindness worldwide. The infection is spread from person to person through direct or indirect contact with infected eyes or nasal discharge. Risk factors for trachoma include poor hygiene, inadequate access to clean water, and the use of unhygienic toilet facilities. In 1996, the WHO launched the WHO Global Trachoma Elimination Program (GET) with the goal of eliminating trachoma as a public health problem by 2020. Other partners in the GET, including the World Bank, have committed to supporting the program's efforts to provide safe and effective interventions to eliminate trachoma. A resolution adopted by the World Health Assembly in 2020, resolution 73(33), set 2030 as a new target date for global trachoma elimination.

translations.source: Izigidi ezingu-152 izingane ezizalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi kushuminyaka elidlule Ezinganeni ezingu-10 ezizalwayo, eyodwa izalwa phambi kwesikhathi futhi emizuzwini engu-40 eyodwa kulezo ngane iyafa Amazinga okuzalwa kwezingane ngaphambi kwesikhathi awakashintshi kushuminyaka eledlule kunoma yisiphi isifunda emhlabeni Imithelela yengxabano,ukuguquka kwesimo sezulu,ingcuphe yokunyuka kweCOVID-19 kubantu besifazane nezingane yonke indawo Isu liyadingeka ngokushesha ukwenza ngcono ukuvikela ukuzalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi, okuhambisana nokunakekela okungcono ezinganeni ezikhahlamezekile kanye nemindeni yazo Isilinganiso sezigindi ezingu-13.4 izingane ezizalwe ngaphambi kwesikhathi nokungafika esigidini esisodwa semithelela yokufa, ngokombiko omusha okhishiwe yi-United Nations ama-ejensi kanye nababambisani namhlanje. Lo mzuliswano wokulingana wengane eyodwa kweziyishumi ezisheshe zizalwe (ngaphambi kwamasonto angu-37 okukhulelwa) emhlabeni wonke. Ukusheshe uzalwe: ishuminyaka lokwenza ekuzalweni ngaohambi kwesikhathi, kukhiqizwe Inhlangano yezempilo emhlabeni (WHO) ne United Nations Children Fund (UNICEF) ndawonye nePMNCH -- ubudlelwane obukhulu emhlabeni babantu besifazane, izingane, nentsha, kuveza ukuuthusa bokuthula kokuphuthumayo kwezokuzalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi, okunesikhathi eside kunganakiwe esikalini sakho sobucayi okuveza ukuqhubeka ekwenzeningcono ezempilo zezingane nokuphila. Umbiko ukubuyekezwa kulinganisa okuphuma kuWHO ne-UNICEF, izilungiselele neLondon School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, ekusabalaleni kokuzalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi. Kukhona konke kutholakala ukuthi amazinga okuzalwa kwezingane ngaphambi kwesikhathi awakashintshi kunoma yisiphi isifunda emhlabeni kuleshuminyaka eledlule nezigidi ezingu-152 zezingane ezihluphekile ezisheshe zazalwa kusuka ngo-2010 kuya ku-2020.
translations.reference: 152 million babies born preterm in the last decade Of every 10 babies born, 1 is preterm – and every 40 seconds, 1 of those babies dies Preterm birth rates have not changed in the past decade in any region of the world The impacts of conflict, climate change, COVID-19 are increasing risks for women and babies everywhere Action is needed urgently to improve the prevention of preterm birth, alongside better care for affected babies and their families An estimated 13.4 million babies were born pre-term in 2020, with nearly 1 million dying from preterm complications, according to a new report released by United Nations agencies and partners today. This is equivalent to around 1 in 10 babies born early (before 37 weeks of pregnancy) worldwide. Born too soon: decade of action on preterm birth, produced by the World Health Organization (WHO) and the United Nations Children’s Fund (UNICEF) together with PMNCH -- the world’s largest alliance for women, children, and adolescents, sounds the alarm on a “silent emergency” of preterm birth, long under-recognized in its scale and severity, which is impeding progress in improving children’s health and survival. The report includes updated estimates from WHO and UNICEF, prepared with the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, on the prevalence of preterm births. Overall, it finds that preterm birth rates have not changed in any region in the world in the past decade, with 152 million vulnerable babies born too soon from 2010 to 2020.
translations.hypothesis: Approximately 152,000 babies were born preterm in the past year, with 10,000 born before 24 weeks of gestation. This represents a significant proportion of all preterm births globally. The rates of preterm birth have not changed significantly in any region of the world in the past year. Factors such as maternal health, socioeconomic status, and access to healthcare can all contribute to preterm birth. There is an urgent need to improve the prevention of preterm birth, which would also improve the health and well-being of mothers and their babies. A new report from the World Health Organization (WHO) and the United Nations Children's Fund (UNICEF), in collaboration with the March of Dimes, highlights the importance of early and effective interventions to prevent preterm birth. The report emphasizes the need for a coordinated global effort to address the issue of preterm birth, which is a major cause of infant mortality and morbidity. The report also calls for increased investment in research and innovation to develop new and more effective interventions to prevent preterm birth.

translations.source: Ukuzalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi manje sekuyimbangela eholayo ekufeni kwezingane,ukubala okungaphezu kuka 1 wokungu 5 wakho konke ukufa okwenzeka ngaphambi kosuku lokuzalwa lweminyaka emihlanu. Abaphilayo kwabazalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi babhekana nemiphumela yezempilo impilo yabo yonke, ngokukhula kokujwayeleka kokukhubazeka kanye nokubambezeleka kokukhula. Izikhala zokuphila yisifunda, imali engenayo, ibala Ukwakhela emmbikweni wesikhonkwane esihlokweni sango-2012,lokhu kusheshe uzalwe, ishuminyaka, umbiko unikeza ukubhekwa okukhulu kokusabalala kokuzalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi nomthelela oqinile kwabesifazane, imiphakathi nezomnotho. Kujwayelekile lapho izingane zizalelwe khona kuzonquma ukuthi zizophila yini. Umbiko uqaphela ukuthi uyedwa kuphela kwabangu-10 abantwana abasheshe bazalwa (<amasonto angu-28) abaphilayo emzaweni anemali encane engenayo,uma uqhathanisa kwangaphezu kwangu-9 ku-10 emazweni anemali ephezulu engenayo. Izikhala zokungalingani ezihlobene nebala, nobuzwe nemali engenayo nokthola ukunakekelwa okufanele kukhomba ukuphila kokusheshe uzalwe ukufa kanye nokukhubazeka ngisho nasemazweni anemali ephezulu engenayo. Southern Asia nesub-Saharan Afrika banamazinga aphezulu okusheshe kuzalwe kulezizizfunda futhi abhekene nengcuphe yokushona kwezingane ephezulu. Ndawonye, lezi zifunda ezimbili zibala ngaphezu kuka-65% wezingane ezisheshe zazalwa emhlabeni. Umbiko uphinde uveze umthelela wengxabano,ukuguquka kwesimo sezulu nokulimala kwendawo, COVID-19 nokunyuka kokubiza kwempilo okunyusa ingcuphe kubantu besifazane nezingane yonke indawo.
translations.reference: Preterm birth is now the leading cause of child deaths, accounting for more than 1 in 5 of all deaths of children occurring before their 5th birthday. Preterm survivors can face lifelong health consequences, with an increased likelihood of disability and developmental delays. Survival gaps by region, income, race Building from a landmark report on the topic in 2012, this new Born too soon “decade” report provides a comprehensive overview of the prevalence of preterm birth and its profound impact on women, families, societies and economies. Too often, where babies are born determines if they survive. The report notes that only 1 in 10 extremely preterm babies (<28 weeks) survive in low-income countries, compared to more than 9 in 10 in high-income countries. Gaping inequalities related to race, ethnicity, income, and access to quality care determine the likelihood of preterm birth, death, and disability, even in high-income countries. Southern Asia and sub-Saharan Africa have the highest rates of preterm birth, and preterm babies in these regions face the highest mortality risk. Together, these two regions account for more than 65% of preterm births globally. The report also highlights that the impacts of conflict, climate change and environmental damage, COVID-19, and rising living costs are increasing risks for women and babies everywhere.
translations.hypothesis: Being born prematurely is a serious health risk for infants, with a mortality rate of over 1 in 5 before the age of 5. Premature babies face a range of health challenges throughout their lives, including breathing difficulties, feeding problems, and developmental delays. The causes of premature birth are varied, including maternal health problems, infections, and complications during pregnancy. In many cases, premature babies are born because their mothers are unable to afford adequate prenatal care. The report notes that premature babies are more likely to experience long-term health problems, even in developed countries. This is because premature babies' bodies are not fully developed, and they may not be able to fight off infections as easily as full-term babies. Southern Asia and sub-Saharan Africa have the highest rates of premature birth in the world, and these regions also have the highest rates of infant mortality. In fact, over 65% of premature babies are born in these two regions. The report also highlights the impact of poverty, lack of access to healthcare, and environmental factors on the health of premature babies.

translations.source: Isibonelo, ukungcola komoya kulinganiselwa ukuthi kunomthelela wezigidi ezingu-6 zokusheshe kuzalwe unyaka ngamunye. Kungafika ku 1 kwabangu 10 izingane ezisheshe zazalwa ezizalwe emazweni abuthaka angu-10 akhahlamezwe izinkinga zabantu ngokokuhlaziya okusha embikweni. Ingcuphe yomama abakhulelwe, njengokukhulelwa kwamatshitshi kusondelene nokusheshe uzale. Lokhu kugqamisa isidingo sokuqinisekisa ukuthola ezempilo zokuzala nezocansi, okufaka ukusebenza kokuhlela umndeni, ukunakekelwa okusezingeni eliphezulu kwabakhulelwe nangesikhathi sokuzala. Ajenda yokwenza: utshalomali oluningi ezweni nokushoshova okuholwa ngabazali Ishuminyaka eledlulesibone ukukhula kwezishoshovu emphakathini ekuvinjweni kokusheshe kuzalwe kwezingane, okuholwa uhlelokuxhumana lwabazali, izifundiswa zempilo, izifundiswa umphakathi nabanye. Ezweni lonke amaqoqo emindeni ekhahlamezekile ngokusheshe kuzalwe kwengane alokhu ephambili ekumeleni ukuthola ukunakekelwa okungcono nokushinthwa kwenqubomgomo nokweseka imindeni. Okuholela kuInternational Maternal Newborn Helath Conference, eKapa eNingizimu Afrika kusuka ngo-8-11 kuNhlaba, iWHO, i-UNICEF, i-UNFPA nePMNCH bamemezela ukulandela izenzo ukwenza ngcono ukunakekela kwabesifazane nabazelwe ubukhulu bezingcuphe okuvela kokuzalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi. Ukunyuswa kotshalomali:ukuhlanganisa izinsizakusebenza zamazwe angaphandle nezendawo ukusiza ezempilo zomama nezingane ezizelwe, ukuqinisekisa ukunakekelwa okuphezulu kuyanikezwa uma noma nalapho kudingeka khona. Ukunyuswa kokwenza: Ukuhlangabezana nokuqondwe ngamazwe kokuqhubeka ngokwenza okusungulwe yinqubomgomo yezwe kokunakekelwa komama nezingane ezizelwe.
translations.reference: For example, air pollution is estimated to contribute to 6 million preterm births each year. Nearly 1 in 10 preterm babies are born in the 10 most fragile countries affected by humanitarian crises, according to a new analysis in the report. Maternal health risks, such as adolescent pregnancy and pre-eclampsia, are closely linked to preterm births. This underlines the need to ensure access to sexual and reproductive health services, including effective family planning, with high-quality care in pregnancy and around the time of birth. Agenda for action: more country investment and parent-led activism The past decade has also seen a growth of community activism on preterm birth and stillbirth prevention, driven by networks of parents, health professionals, academia, civil society, and others. Throughout the world, groups for affected families of preterm birth have been at the forefront of advocating for access to better care and policy change and supporting other families. Leading up to the International Maternal Newborn Health Conference, in Cape Town, South Africa from 8-11 May, WHO, UNICEF, UNFPA and PMNCH are calling for the following actions to improve care for women and newborns and mitigate risks from preterm births: Increased investments: Mobilizing international and domestic resources to optimize maternal and newborn health, ensuring high quality care is provided when and where it is needed. Accelerated implementation: Meeting country targets for progress through implementing established national policies for maternal and newborn care.
translations.hypothesis: For example, the impact of premature birth is estimated to be around 6 million deaths per year globally. This represents approximately 11% of all newborn deaths. Factors contributing to premature birth include maternal health conditions, such as pre-eclampsia and gestational diabetes. This highlights the need for improved maternal and newborn health services, including family planning, antenatal care, and skilled birth attendance. A key strategy is to increase access to essential health services for mothers and newborns, particularly in resource-limited settings. This includes strengthening health systems, training healthcare providers, and promoting community-based interventions. In addition, there is a growing need for research on the causes and consequences of premature birth, as well as the development of new interventions to prevent and treat it. This led to the International Maternal Newborn Health Conference, held in Cape Town, South Africa, from 8-11 November, where the WHO, UNICEF, UNFPA, and PMNCH committed to taking action to improve the care of mothers and newborns at all stages of the life course. One key area of focus is to increase access to essential health services for mothers and newborns, particularly in resource-limited settings. This includes strengthening health systems, training healthcare providers, and promoting community-based interventions.

translations.source: Ukuhlanganisa kuyona yonke imikhakha: Ukugqugquzela ezemfundo ngempilo, ukweseka utshalomali lwezomnotho oluhlakaniphile, nokubambisana kwezimali kuyona yonke imikhakha, ukuqinisa ukuhambisana kwesimo sezulu nokuphendula kuyona yonke impilo, ukwenza ngcono ukuxhumana nokubekezela ezinhlelweni eziphuthumayo. Okwenziwa abendawo okusha: ukutshala endaweni okuholwa okusha nocwaningo lokweseka intuthuko yokunakekelwa okuhle nokuthola ngokulingana. Okucashuniwe: uSolwazi Joy Lawn, London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine Omunye wabaholayoUsheshe wazalwa-ishuminyaka lwesenzo ekushesheni uzalwe (2023) noKusheshe uzalwe (2012) "Lombiko omusha ukhombisa ukuthi ukubiza kokungenzi kulelishuminyaka eledlule kwakuyizigidi ezingu-152 ezisheshe zazalwa. Ngesikhathi kwezinye izifunda bekhahlamezeke kakhulu ukusheshe uzale kusabisa ukuqhubeka kwezempilo kuwona wonke amazwe. Utahlomali olukhulu ekunakekelweni kwabazelwe ababuthaka kungasindisa izigidi zemindeni ekuphukeni kwenhliziyo. Umsebenzi omuningi uyadingeka ukugwema ukusheshe kuzalwe, okuzophinde kwenze ngcono ukuqhubeka kokwehlisa ukufa komama nezingane ezisheshe zazalwa. Ndawonye lokhu okubili kokuvikela ukusheshe kuzalwe nokunakekelwa kuzokhiqiza abantu abanempilo nemiphakathi ukuletha kwezomnotho kanye nokuthuthukiswa kwenhlalakahle. Isizukulwane sethu esizayo sincike kuthina kukhona konke esikwenzayo manje-utshalomali ngeke lube luncane kodwa izimbuyiselo kulolutshalomali luzoba lukhulu kakhulu wonke amazwe"
translations.reference: Integration across sectors: Promoting education through the life cycle; supporting smarter economic investments, with co-financing across sectors; strengthening climate adaptation responses across the life-course; and advancing the coordination and resilience of emergency systems. Locally driven innovation: Investing in locally led innovation and research to support improvements in quality of care and equity in access. Quotes: Prof. Joy Lawn, London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine Co-lead, Born too soon: decade of action on preterm birth (2023) and Born too soon (2012) "This new report shows that the cost of inaction over the last decade was 152 million babies born too soon. While some regions are more affected, preterm birth threatens health progress in every country. Greater investment in the care of vulnerable newborns can save millions of families from heartbreak. More work is also needed to prevent preterm birth, which will also improve progress in reducing stillbirths and maternal deaths. Together these twin tracks of preterm prevention and care will produce healthier individuals and societies to deliver on economic and social development. Our next generation depends on us all acting now -- the investment may not be small but the returns on this investment will be major for every country"
translations.hypothesis: Collaboration is essential across all sectors: advocating for evidence-based policies, promoting sustainable economic development, and fostering financial inclusion are all crucial across all sectors. Strengthening climate resilience and adaptation is vital for all communities. Improving communication and preparedness in emergency situations is also essential. New initiatives: establishing a new research center for maternal and child health, and conducting a comprehensive review of existing policies. Key findings: Dr. Joy Lawn, London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine "The new data shows that over 152,000 women gave birth in hospitals in 2023. In contrast, the number of births at home has declined in many countries. The high cost of hospital births can be a barrier to access for low-income families. More research is needed to understand the factors that contribute to the decline in home births, and to develop strategies to promote safe and affordable home birth options. By protecting women's right to choose where to give birth, we can improve maternal and child health outcomes, and promote economic development and social equity. The future of our society depends on the choices we make today. Investment in maternal and child health will pay off in the long run, leading to a healthier and more prosperous society for all."

translations.source: uDkt Nahya Salim,waseTanzania: Ukusheshe uzalwe omunye wababhali nezwe eliholwa ngeNewborn Essentials Solutions and Technology 360 (NEST360) "Asisekho manje isizathu sokuthula. Manje sinamadivayisi ezandleni zethu kanye nolwazi lukushintsha imiphumela yezingane zethu ezihluphekile ezizelwe. Ngiyaziqhenya ukubona uhulumeni wethu manje etshala futhi ezibophezela ukwenza yonke indawo ngisho nasemakhaya. Ishuminyaka elizayo lingaba futhi kumele lihluke kulabo ababhekene zokusheshe bazale lana nakuyona yonke indawo." UDkt Anshu Banerjee, uMqondisi wabakhulelwe, abazelwe, izingane nentsha kwezempilo kanye nokuguga eWHO "Ukuqinisekisa ukunakekelwa okufanele kwabancane, abantekenteke nemindeni yabo kufanele impela nokwenzangcono ezempilo nokuphila komntwana. Inqubekela phambili iyadingeka futhi ukusiza ukuvikela ukusheshe bazale-lokhu kusho ukuthi wonke umuntu wesifazane kumele akwazi ukuthola ezempilo ezifanele ngaphambi nangesikhathi sokukhulelwa ukuze kuhlonzwe futhi kuphathwe izingcuphe." USteven Lauwerier, uMqondisi weZempilo (a.i.), e-UNICEF "Ngemva kokufa kokusheshe uzalwe njalo ukulandelana kokulahlekelwa nokuphuka kwenhliziyo.
translations.reference: Dr Nahya Salim, Tanzania: Born Too Soon co-author and national lead of Newborn Essential Solutions and Technologies 360 (NEST360) “There is now no excuse for silence. We now have in our hands the devices and the knowledge to change outcomes for our most vulnerable newborns. I am proud to see my government now investing and committed to implementation across even rural areas. The next decade can and must be different for those facing preterm birth here and everywhere.” Dr Anshu Banerjee, Director for Maternal, Newborn, Child and Adolescent Health and Ageing at WHO “Ensuring quality care for these tiniest, most vulnerable babies and their families is absolutely imperative for improving child health and survival. Progress is also needed to help prevent preterm births -- this means every woman must be able to access quality health services before and during pregnancy to identify and manage risks.” Steven Lauwerier, Director of Health (a.i.), UNICEF “After every preterm death is a trail of loss and heartbreak.
translations.hypothesis: Dr. Nahya Salim, from Tanzania: "We are now in a situation where there is no longer a reason to delay. We now have the technology and knowledge to safely deliver babies at home. I am concerned that the government is now pushing for all births to take place in hospitals, even in rural areas. I believe that the next few years will see a significant increase in the number of home births, and that this is a positive development." Dr. Anshu Banerjee, Director of Maternal and Child Health at WHO "Ensuring that all babies, mothers, and families receive the care they need is essential for improving child health and well-being. We need to continue to work to reduce the number of preventable deaths from childbirth, which means that all women should have access to safe and affordable healthcare before and during pregnancy." Steven Lauwerier, Acting Director of Health, UNICEF "Following the tragic loss of a baby due to childbirth, there is a need to prevent further deaths and injuries.

translations.source: Ngaphandle kobungcono okuningi emhlabeni esikwenzile eshumininyaka eledlule, asikenzi nqubekela phambili ekwehliseni inamba yezingane ezincane ezisheshe zizalwe noma sivimbe ingcuphe ekuzalweni kwabo. Loluhambo luyakhubaza. Yisikhathi sokwenza ngcono ukuthola ukunakekelwa komama abakhulelwe kanye nezingane ezizalwe kungashayi isikhathi nokuqinisekisa ukuthi yonke ingane ithola ukuqala kwezempilo nokuphokophela empilweni." UHelga Fogstad, uMqondisi Omkhulu, wePMNCH Ukusheshe uzalwe kusho incazelo yokuthi kumele sinyuse utshalomali nesibophezelo kwabasheshe bazalwa - imbangela enkulu emhlabeni yokufa kwezingane ezingaphansi kweminyaka emihlanu. Ukuqhubeka kokulinganisa kumama nezempilo zomntwana ozelwe, kanye nokuvikelwa kokuzalwa kwabantwana kungakashayi isikhathi, manje sekubekwe emuva ngokuhlanganiswa okukhathazayo kweCOVID-19, ukuguquka kwesimo sezulu, ukwandisa izingxabano nokukhulisa izindleko zokuphila. Ngokusebenza ndawonye ngokubambisana - uhulumeni, abaxhasi, umkhakha ozimele, umphakathi, abazali, izifundiswa zempilo - singaveza inkinga ngokuthula kokuphuthuma nokuletha ukuvikelwa kokuzalwa ngaphambi kwesikhathi nemizamo yokunakelewa phambili kwezempilo yezwe nemizamo yokuthuthukisa, ukwakha amandla omuntu ngokweseka imindeni, imiphakathi nezomnotho yonke indawo. "UGabriela noJerome Foster, e-Las Vegas, e-United States: abazali baJalen Foster, abasunguli beJalen Gift Foundation "Indawo yethu yasungulwa ngemva kokushona komfana wethu, uJalen Foster, owasheshe wazalwa enamasonto angu-27 ngenxa yokusheshe ngisikwe ngegciwane elingaziwa njengeqoqo B streptococcus. Ukulahlekelwa yindodana yethu kwasifakela ingcindezi futhi kwakungekho ukwesekwa emphakathini nokuthi ngesikhathi sinquma ukunikezela ngendawo ukududuza nokunika ithemba emindenini elahlekelwe ngumntwana.
translations.reference: Despite the many advances the world has made in the past decade, we have made no progress in reducing the number of small babies born too soon or averting the risk of their death. The toll is devasting. It’s time we improve access to care for pregnant mothers and preterm infants and ensure every child gets a healthy start and thrives in life.” Helga Fogstad, Executive Director, PMNCH “Born too soon underlines why we must increase investment and accountability for preterm birth – the world’s largest cause of death of children under the age of five. Progress is flatlining for maternal and newborn health, as well as the prevention of stillbirths, and is now pushed back further through the devastating combination of COVID-19, climate change, expanding conflicts and rising living costs. “By working together in partnership – governments, donors, the private sector, civil society, parents, and health professionals – we can sound the alarm about this “silent emergency”, and bring preterm prevention and care efforts to the forefront of national health and development efforts, building human capital by supporting families, societies and economies everywhere. ”Gabriela and Jerome Foster, Las Vegas, United States: parents of Jalen Foster, and founders of Jalen’s Gift Foundation “Our foundation was founded after the loss of our son, Jalen Foster, who was a preterm stillbirth at 27 weeks due to prenatal onset of a bacteria known as group B streptococcus. Losing our son was devastating, and there was no support in the community and that’s when we decided to provide a place of comfort and hope for families who have lost a baby.
translations.hypothesis: Despite many positive advancements made in the past year, we still face significant challenges in ensuring access to quality maternal and newborn care for all mothers and babies, particularly in low-resource settings. This is a critical issue. It is time to prioritize improving the care of mothers and newborns, and to ensure that every child has the opportunity to thrive. Helga Fogstad, Director, PMNCH (Progress on Maternal, Newborn and Child Health) "Neonatal mortality refers to the death of a newborn within the first 28 days of life. Addressing this issue requires a multi-faceted approach, including increased investment in maternal and newborn care, improved access to essential services, and stronger health systems. However, progress has been hampered by a number of factors, including the COVID-19 pandemic, climate change, conflict, and rising costs of living. By working together – governments, healthcare providers, civil society, families, and communities – we can overcome these challenges and ensure that all mothers and babies have the chance to live healthy and fulfilling lives. Gabriela and Jerome Foster, Las Vegas, United States: The parents of Jalen Foster, founder of the Jalen Gift Foundation "Our foundation was established after the tragic death of our son, Jalen Foster, who was stillborn at 27 weeks due to a Streptococcus infection. The loss of our child was devastating, and we felt a need to create a space where other families who have experienced the same pain could find support and hope.

translations.source: Besingafuni eminye imindeni izezwe njengba sazizwa - uwedwa. Ngakho, siqale iJalen's Gift Foundation ukusiza imindeni elahlekelwe, ukubanikeza usizo lwamahhala oluningi, njengezithombe, ukwesekwa kokuzila nokubasiza ezinhlelweni zesikhumbuzo." ***Landa usheshe wazalwa kuzinsizakusebenza zezindaba
translations.reference: We didn’t want other families to feel like we did – alone. So, we started Jalen’s Gift Foundation to help other families going through a loss, to provide multiple free services, such as photograph, grief support and help towards memorial services.” ***Download the Born too soon media resources
translations.hypothesis:
We didn't want any other families to feel as alone as we did. So, we started the Jalen's Gift Foundation to help families in need, providing them with free resources such as food, clothing, and support in grief counseling.
